message:
fix(raptorq): move symbol authentication to receiver boundary, add strict/permissive modes

Receiver-side authentication redesign:
- Previously, symbol authentication was delegated to the DecodingPipeline
  via verify_auth in DecodingConfig. This meant the decoder had to know
  about security contexts, and there was no way to distinguish "verified
  but failed" from "never checked" in the ReceiveOutcome.

- Now the RaptorQReceiver authenticates each target-object symbol at the
  receive boundary before feeding it to the decoder. This gives the
  receiver full control over the fail-closed vs. fail-open decision:
  - Strict mode (default): a symbol with a bad authentication tag
    immediately returns ErrorKind::CorruptedSymbol, failing the entire
    receive. This prevents an attacker from injecting forged repair
    symbols that could corrupt the decoded output.
  - Permissive mode: a symbol with a bad tag is still fed to the
    decoder, but ReceiveOutcome::authenticated is set to false so
    callers know the decoded data may be tainted.

- The `authenticated` field on ReceiveOutcome now accurately reflects
  whether ALL consumed symbols were cryptographically verified, rather
  than just whether a SecurityContext was present.

- DecodingPipeline's SymbolAcceptResult::Rejected(AuthenticationFailed)
  is now explicitly matched at the receiver level to fail closed in
  strict mode—previously it fell through to the generic Rejected arm
  and was silently ignored.

RFC 6330 tuple_indices defensive assertions:
- Add precondition asserts for zero degrees (d, d1), zero steps (a, a1),
  and out-of-range start indices (b, b1). Previously, zero-degree tuples
  would produce one spurious index (the start index), and zero-step
  tuples would produce degenerate duplicate walks—both silent correctness
  bugs that could only manifest from a broken tuple generator.
- Two new test functions verify the assertions fire on invalid inputs.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
